## [00:00 - 03:02]

Okay, this is a case study for David H. Lawrence the 17th. This is a joint venture launch that he did. Now David is an actor, he's a voice talent, he's been a radio host, he's been an online entrepreneur for a number of years, and he's best known as the puppet master on the NBC sci-fi TV series Heroes. So David's done a lot of different things, I've actually known him for a long time, and he did a G.E.V. launch. It's an online course on how to be successful voiceover artist. And a little background, he says, I've been launching, I've been kind of launching, but not wholeheartedly for the eight years or so that I've been teaching my course. I had won a number of awards for the class and I decided to get serious about it. So he did one serious NANJV launch that did $51,000, which was pretty awesome considering before that he just had occasional sales here and there that came in for the first eight years. So he had it on the market for eight years, didn't do much, then he put together a real launch for his list, which is relatively small, and he netted $51,000, so that's really cool. Then he organized a joint venture launch. He got together a group of partners, not large, a small group of partners, who says are adjacent to him in the industry, and he not grossing $137,000. So that was more than two times, almost three times what he'd done in his internal launch. And he paid out $40,000 to his JV partners and more than doubled his list size. So that's pretty cool. That's the power of a JV launch. If you go from $51,231,000 or $137,000. So he had six partners, so you don't need hundreds of partners, his one partner, number one did 11 sales, number two did five sales, number three did two sales, and then he had three others that did one sale each. So he had six total partners, 21 total sales. Now, the interesting thing here is this split ends up, when you're in the JV world, the joint venture world, the affiliate launch world, what you'll find is your top partners tend to get your, it's like the 80-20 rule, where 80% of the sales will come from 20% of your partners, but really it's like the 90-10 rule, where 90% of the sales will come from two of your partners. So here, his top two partners did 16 of the 21 sales. So his top two partners, so top two out of six,

## [03:02 - 06:03]

he might have had other partners, I'm not positive, he might have had additional partners that didn't do any sales, but really it was the top partners that made all the difference, and that's the way it is in almost every joint venture launch I've been part of. So the thing is, is you don't need lots and lots of partners, you just need a few good partners. And you wanna focus your effort on the really good partners, and once who will be your top partners. Now, the caveat to that is sometimes when you're doing, first doing a JV launch, you actually don't know who your top partners are gonna be. ## [06:03 - 09:03]

he reached out one-on-one, either by phone or email or both. And that's the way this world works. That's the way the joint venture world works.
